steps.title: Step 1 — Identify the task
steps.content: Quantitative completion. The hypothesis: holding other factors constant, as governments become MORE efficient, GDP-per-capita growth becomes MORE important to urban land expansion than population growth. The blank must use graph data to extend that proposal.

steps.title: Step 2 — Apply the hypothesis
steps.content: The graph shows that for Region 1, urban land expansion was driven LESS by GDP per capita (and more by population) in 2000–2014 than in 1970–2000. By the contrapositive of the proposal — if GDP's importance fell, efficiency must have fallen — this implies Region 1's national governments became LESS efficient in the later period.

steps.title: Step 3 — Per-choice elimination
steps.content:
• Choice B — countries in Region 1 experienced a slower rate of economic growth in the period from 2000 to 2014 than countries in Region 2 did, despite increasing national government efficiency in Region 1.: introduces an economic-growth comparison the data don't address and contradicts the inferred direction of efficiency in Region 1. ✗
  • Choice C — national governments of most countries in Region 2 became more efficient in the period from 2000 to 2014 than they had been in the period from 1970 to 2000, but those of several countries in this region did not.: focuses only on Region 2 and on within-region exceptions; doesn't reflect what the Region 1 data imply under the hypothesis. ✗
  • Choice D — national governments of countries in Region 1 and in Region 2 generally became more efficient in the period from 2000 to 2014 than they had been in the period from 1970 to 2000, but at different rates.: contradicts the Region 1 inference that efficiency declined; overstates a uniform increase the graph doesn't support. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
